segments.s523-g49: This is a compact noun phrase: همان (demonstrative) + ماه‌رخ (moon-faced, i.e. beautiful person/face) followed by the prepositional phrase بر دگر بارگی ('on/upon another leaf/foliage'). In Persian poetry the noun ماه‌رخ can stand for person(s) with a moonlike face and the plurality of the verb is inferred; دگر modifies بارگی (= 'another/other leaf'). Word order is Demonstrative + Noun + Preposition + Modifier + Noun.
segments.s523-g50: نشستند و رفتند is a coordinated past-tense clause with plural verbs: نشستند (they sat/alighted) and رفتند (they departed/left). The subject is carried over from the previous phrase (the moon-faced ones). یکبارگی is a postverbal adverb meaning 'suddenly/at once' and scopes over the coordinated verbs, so the sequence means they sat (alighted) and immediately left.
segments.s523-g51: A framed motion/attention phrase: از ایوان ('from the portico') + سوی پارس ('toward Pars') followed by the verb بنهاد and its object/phrase روی ('face'). بنهاد is a past-tense verb here meaning 'he set/placed' (poetically 'he set his face = he turned his face toward'); روی functions as the thing set (i.e. 'his face'). Word order places source and direction before the verb, then the object 'روی'.
segments.s523-g52: همی is an old/poetic preverb marking continuous or ongoing aspect ('kept on' / 'proceeded to'); همی رفت therefore means 'he went on / proceeded'. The following phrase شادان دل و راه‌جوی describes his state: شادان modifies دل ('with a joyful heart') and راه‌جوی is a coordinating noun/adjective 'and seeking the road' (i.e. a traveler). The whole clause is a simple past narrative with an adjectival phrase describing manner.
segments.s523-g33: همی بود is a habitual/continuous past construction meaning ‘he remained’ (همی = still/continuous, بود = past ‘was’). تا introduces a temporal limit (‘until’) and is followed by the clause شب برآمد ز کوه (‘night rose from the mountain’), where برآمد is the past verb ‘arose’ and ز + کوه is the poetic prepositional phrase ‘from the mountain’.
segments.s523-g34: بخفت (past) ‘lay down / slept’ precedes the name اردوان (verb–subject order is common in poetry). جای شد literally ‘the place became’ (شد = become) is an impersonal/resultative expression meaning the seat/place was left vacant, and بی‌گروه is the adjective ‘without company’.
segments.s523-g35: از ایوان بیامد is the simple past ‘came from the iwan’ (از = from; ایوان = iwan/porch; بیامد = came). به کردار تیر is a simile ‘in the manner of an arrow’ where به introduces the manner and کردار تیر specifies how he came (swiftly/straight as an arrow).
segments.s523-g36: بیاورد is the narrative past ‘brought’ (here ‘he brought’); گوهر is the direct object ‘the jewel/gem’, and بر اردشیر is a poetic dative/prepositional phrase ‘to Ardashir’ (بر here functions like به meaning ‘to/toward’). Word order is verb–object–prepositional phrase.
segments.s523-g37: جهانجوی را دید is ‘he saw the world-seeker’ with را marking the definite direct object. جامی به دست is an appositive/absolute phrase ‘a cup in (his) hand’ describing that person; به here marks the location/instrument (‘in/with the hand’).
segments.s523-g38: نگهبان اسپان is a genitive construction ‘the guards (keepers) of the horses’ (نگهبان taking اسپان as its object/possessed). همه quantifies them (‘all’). خفته مست are two predicative adjectives ‘asleep, drunk’; the copula is omitted (understood from context), a common narrative ellipsis.
segments.s523-g39: کجا is the interrogative adverb ‘where’ placed frontally. مستشان کرده بود is a past-perfect construction (‘had made them drunk’) using کرده بود (کرده = done/made; بود = had/was) and the clitic شان on مست indicates ‘them’. اردشیر at the end is the agent ‘Ardashir’.
segments.s523-g40: که introduces the result/explanatory clause ‘that…’. وی ‘he’ is the subject; خواست + رفتن is a past-periphrastic expression ‘desired/intended to go’ (خواست = wanted; رفتن = to go). همی here is an emphatic/continuous particle and ناگزیر is a predicative adjective ‘compelled/inevitable’, so the clause means ‘that he was compelled to go’.
segments.s523-g1: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the subordinate clause; شد is the past of شدن 'became' and precedes the nominal phrase as is common in poetic word order; روی کشور is a nominal phrase 'the face (appearance) of the realm' (روی = face, کشور = realm); به کردار قیر is a prepositional/comparative complement 'in the manner of tar' (کردار here used as 'manner/way' or 'conduct', and قیر = tar), describing how the realm's face became.
segments.s523-g2: کنیزک is the subject 'the young maidservant'; بیامد is simple past 'came' (poetic past form); بر + proper name (بر اردشیر) functions as a directional/prepositional phrase 'to Ardashir' (بر here = toward/to).
segments.s523-g3: چو = 'when' introducing the clause; دریا is the grammatical subject 'the sea'; برآشفت is a past-intransitive verb 'rose up/grew agitated' (poetic verb before its nominative); مرد جوان follows as the nominative/appositive 'the young man' (jargon of epic poetry often places the verb before the noun it concerns).
segments.s523-g4: که is a relative 'who/that' attaching this clause to 'مرد جوان'; یک روز 'for one day' is an adverbial time phrase; نشکیبی is formed with the negative prefix ن- + شکیبی and here denotes lack of patience (i.e., 'impatience' or 'was not patient'); از + name (از اردوان) means 'with regard to/with Ardavan' (i.e., directed at Ardavan).
segments.s523-g5: کنیزک 'the maidservant' is the subject; بگفت (بِـ + گفت) is the past narrative 'said' / 'told'; آنچ is a relativa/indefinite pronoun 'that which' (introducing the content of her speech); روشن‌روان is an epithet 'the bright‑soul' (a person described by that epithet) — syntactically the phrase means she spoke what that person had said.
segments.s523-g6: همی is an adverb/prefix indicating continuing or habitual action ('was continually / kept'); گفت here functions as the verbal idea 'said' (poetic usage) and با نامدار اردوان is the prepositional phrase 'to/with renowned Ardavan' specifying the addressee. (Note: همی + گفت conveys an ongoing or repeated speech act in epic style.)
segments.s523-g7: سخن 'the speech/words' is the theme; چون here functions 'as/when' marking the temporal/simile sense; ز گلنار 'from Golnār' identifies the speaker/source (گلنار is a female name here); زان سان is a poetic adverbial 'in that manner/so'; شنید is simple past 'he heard' — standard verb-final placement.
segments.s523-g8: شکیبایی 'patience' and خامشی 'silence' are the two nouns joined by و 'and'; برگزید is the past verb 'chose/selected' taking the preceding coordinated nouns as its object (poetic word order frequently places objects before the verb).
segments.s523-g17: چنین داد پاسخ is an inverted, literary phrasing meaning "thus gave (an) answer" (چنین = thus; داد is the past of دادن "gave"), and که introduces reported speech. In the clause من بنده‌ام the noun بنده carries the predication and the enclitic -ام functions as the 1st‑person copula, so the literal structure is "I [am] a servant."
segments.s523-g18: نباشم is the negative of باشم (1st person), here functioning as a volitive/confirmatory negative meaning roughly "I will not / I shall not be" or "may I not be" in older poetic usage. جدا از تو is a prepositional phrase 'apart from you' (جدا + از + تو). تا زنده‌ام uses تا = 'until / as long as' plus زنده + enclitic ام = 'I am alive', giving the temporal limit 'as long as I live.'
segments.s523-g19: همی + past stem گفت is a classical Persian imperfective/iterative construction meaning 'kept saying' or 'was saying repeatedly' (همی indicates continuous or habitual aspect). با لب پر از باد سرد is an instrumental/adjunct phrase 'with lips full of cold wind' where پر از = 'full of' and the adjective سرد follows the noun phrase as in classical Persian.
segments.s523-g20: فرو ریخت is a compound verbal phrase (فرو adverbial + past ریخت) meaning 'fell/poured down' (a downward, consummated action). از دیدگان is 'from the eyes' (از + plural دیدگان), and آب زرد is a noun + adjective sequence 'yellow water' (here a poetic reference to tears); the adjective زرد follows the noun as usual.
segments.s523-g21: چنین گفت introduces what was said ('thus said') and با marks the addressee: با ماه‌روی اردشیر = 'to the moon‑faced Ardashir.' ماه‌روی is a compound adjective ('moon‑faced' = beautiful‑faced) modifying the proper name that follows.
segments.s523-g22: که introduces the content clause. فردا is a temporal adverb 'tomorrow' (or 'in the near future'). بباید is a modal/periphrastic form meaning 'must/ought (to),' followed by شدن (infinitive/masdar 'to happen / to become') as its complement; ناگزیر is the predicate/adverbial 'inevitable' placed at the end. Together they mean 'that tomorrow it must inevitably happen.'
segments.s523-g23: بیامد is the past simple 'came.' به introduces the destination (به ایوان = 'to the iwan/porch'), and خویش is expressed as خوی + enclitic ش: خوی (root meaning kinship/own) + ش (possessive enclitic 'his/her/its') together = 'her own.' The diminutive کنیزک marks her as a young maidservant.
segments.s523-g24: This is an absolute/participial‑style clause: به کف برنهاده is a verbal adjective phrase meaning 'having placed (into) the palm' (به + کف + برنهاده). تن و جان خویش 'her body and soul' is the direct object of that verbal phrase; as above خوی + ش marks 'her own.' The sequence describes an act of offering or laying down body and soul (poetic idiom).
segments.s523-g41: The phrase begins with the numeral + noun (دو اسپ = 'two steeds'); the adjective گرانمایه follows the noun in poetic order ('precious/noble'). کرده گزین is a compound verbal expression: کرده is a perfective/participial element and گزین (here not imperative) functions as the lexical verb 'to choose/select', so together they mean 'had chosen/selected'.
segments.s523-g42: بر آخور is a prepositional phrase 'on/at the manger' (بر + آخور). چنان بود is a copular clause 'was thus/so' (چنان 'thus' + بود past 'was'). در زیر زین repeats locative elements ('در' + 'زیر' + 'زین') to mean 'in/under the saddle'—the doubled preposition is emphatic and typical of poetic diction.
segments.s523-g43: Subject (جهانجوی) is followed by the conjunction/temporal چـون used as 'when/as', then the object phrase روی گلنار ('the face of Golnār') and the verb دید at the end—typical SOV order in Persian poetry. جهانجوی is an epithet/noun referring to the person who looks for the world (here, 'the world-seeker' or 'an ambitious man').
segments.s523-g44: همان is a demonstrative 'that/the same' pointing to a previously noted object; گوهر و سرخ دینار lists two objects joined by و ('and'): 'a gem (گوهر)' and 'a red dinar' where سرخ is the adjective modifying دینار. دید repeats the verb for emphasis 'he saw'.
segments.s523-g45: هم‌اندر زمان پیش is a temporal adverbial phrase 'at the same time / meanwhile' (هم‌اندر = 'also/meanwhile', زمان = 'time', پیش = 'before/then'). بنهاد is a past tense verb meaning 'placed' or 'set (forth/offered)' and جام is the object 'cup'.
segments.s523-g46: بزد is the past-tense verb 'he struck'. The phrase is: he struck (بزد) the لگام (bridle) بر سر (upon the head(s) of) تازی اسپان ('Arabian horses'), where تازی qualifies اسپان. Word order places the verb first for poetic effect but the object لگام appears finally.
segments.s523-g47: بپوشید here carries the sense 'covered/lay down' (poetic usage) and together with خفتان (infinitive/noun 'sleep') yields 'they covered themselves and slept' or simply 'they lay down to sleep'. و خود بر نشست connects with contrastive خود 'he himself' + بر نشست 'sat upon/mounted' (نشست = 'sat/settled').
segments.s523-g48: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain' or 'one', modifying تیغ 'sword'. زهر آب‌داده is a compound modifier meaning 'poison-soaked / poisoned' (زهر 'poison' + آب‌داده 'water-given/treated' forming a poetic description of a poisoned blade). به دست is a locative phrase 'in (his) hand'.
segments.s523-g9: دل مرد برنا شد is a nominal phrase with subject–predicate order: دلِ مرد (the man's heart) is understood as the topic, then برنا شد (became youthful/quick) is the verbal predicate ('شد' = 'became'). از + noun here expresses cause/agental source: ازِ ماه تیز = 'because of / by the (sharp) moon' (a poetic image: the moon = the beloved or a piercing glance). The whole clause says the man's heart was altered (became 'برنا') by the moon/beauty.
segments.s523-g10: ازان پس is a temporal adverbial ('from that time on'); همی is an aspectual particle meaning 'continually/kept on'; جست here is a perfect/past-stem used with the sense 'seek' (classical Persian frequently uses جستن for 'seek'); راه گریز is the direct object (a compound noun phrase 'a way of escape'). Word order is adverbial – particle – verb – object.
segments.s523-g11: بدو is a prepositional phrase 'to him' (literally 'unto him'); گفت‌گر is a poetic reporting formula (used to introduce reported speech, roughly 'he said'); من is the first‑person subject; به ایران شوم uses شوم (subjunctive/optative of شدن) to express a prospective or hypothetical state — here best rendered 'if I should be in / go to Iran.' The clause introduces a conditional/intentional statement.
segments.s523-g12: ز ری is the origin/starting-point 'from Rey' (ز = 'from'); سوی is the directional preposition 'toward'; شهر دلیران is the destination noun phrase 'the city of the brave' (دلیران is a plural attributive); شوم repeats the optative/indicative-of‑becoming (I shall be / I will go). The sequence gives a motion/directional clause built on 'from X toward Y' with شوم marking the speaker's intended/resulting state.
segments.s523-g13: تو با من is a topical frame 'you with me' (you accompany me). سگالی is a classical verb meaning 'to set dogs / to hunt with hounds' (here imperative/indicative sense: join in the hunting/ambush); که آیی is a subordinate clause introduced by که with آیی (subjunctive/present 'you come' or 'you appear') and به راه gives the location 'on the road'. The structure is an instruction: accompany me in setting the dogs so that you will come/appear on the road.
segments.s523-g14: گر is the conditional 'if'; ایدر (اینجا) means 'here'; بباشی is the subjunctive/imperative form of بودن ('you should be / you remain'); به نزدیک شاه is a prepositional locative phrase 'near the king.' Grammatically this is a conditional clause: 'If you remain here, (then)...' that ties to the preceding instruction.
segments.s523-g15: اگر introduces a protasis 'if'; با من آیی uses آیی (present/subjunctive of آمدن) 'you come with me'; توانگر is a predicate adjective/noun 'wealthy'; شوی is the 2nd‑person singular of شدن ('you will become'). The clause is a straightforward conditional promise: 'If you come with me, you shall become rich.'
segments.s523-g16: همان is an emphatic demonstrative 'that very / indeed'; بر سر کشور is an idiomatic locution 'over the realm' (بر = 'over/upon', سر = 'head/top/chief' used metaphorically for authority); افسر شوی uses افسر (literally 'helmet' but here synecdochic for 'commander' or 'high officer') and شوی (become). Together it promises the addressee will attain rulership/command — 'you will don the helmet over the country' meaning you will be made commander/ruler.
segments.s523-g25: 'چو' is the temporal conjunction 'when'; 'شد' is the past of شدن 'became'; 'روی گیتی' is a noun phrase 'the face (روی) of the world (گیتی)'; 'ز خورشید زرد' = 'yellow from the sun' (ز = 'from/by', خورشید = 'sun', زرد = adjective 'yellow'). Word order is poetic but literal: 'When became the face of the world yellow from the sun.'
segments.s523-g26: 'به خم' = 'into/onto the curve/arch' (به = 'to/into', خم = 'curve, bend' used poetically for horizon or arch); 'اندر آمد' is an archaic/periphrastic past meaning 'entered/came in'; the verb precedes its subject here (poetic order): 'اندر آمد شب لاژورد' = 'the lapis‑blue night came in.' 'لاژورد' is used adjectivally 'lapis/azure.'
segments.s523-g27: Subject first: 'کنیزک' (the maid). 'در گنجها' marks the place/objects ('in/among the treasures'). 'باز کرد' is the verb in past tense; here 'باز' functions with کرد as 'opened' (بازکردن = to open) rather than 'again'. The object (the chests/treasures) is implied by the prepositional phrase.
segments.s523-g28: 'ز هر گوهری' = 'from/among every jewel' (ز = 'from/of', هر = 'every', گوهری = 'jewel'). 'جستن' is the infinitive 'to search/seek' and 'آغاز کرد' is a light-verb construction 'began (to...)' (آغاز کردن + infinitive = 'to begin to do something'). So the whole clause means 'she began to search among every jewel.'
segments.s523-g29: ز here is the poetic preposition 'of / from' introducing material or possession. The phrase 'یاقوت وز گوهر شاهوار' uses an archaic fused conjunction/preposition (وز = و + ز) meaning 'and of', so the structure is 'of ruby and of (lofty) gem'. The adjective 'شاهوار' follows the noun 'گوهر' as a poetic modifier ('lofty/regal jewel').
segments.s523-g30: ز دینار = 'of/due to dinars' (prepositional phrase). چندانک is a degree adverb 'to that extent / so much' that modifies the following predicate. بودش is past tense 'was' with the enclitic -ش referring to the third person (roughly 'it/that was to him' → 'he had'); here it functions impersonally to mean 'he had / it served him'. به کار literally 'in use' means 'useful / of use' — together the clause says the extent of his use in dinars.
segments.s523-g31: بیامد is the simple past third-person 'he came'. 'به جایی' is the prepositional phrase 'to a place'. که introduces a relative clause 'where/that'. بودش is the past copula with enclitic -ش pointing to the person ('he was / he had been') and here works with نشست to indicate the action of sitting down — overall 'to a place where he sat (down)'.
segments.s523-g32: بدان is built from the preposition ب ('to/into') plus an archaic demonstrative element (here written as دان); together it means 'into/into that' or 'in that'. خانه 'house/room' is the location. بنهاد is past 'he placed/put' (بنهاد == 'he set/placed'). 'گوهر ز دست' = 'the jewel from the hand' — the preposition ز marks source ('from his hand'), so the whole phrase means he placed the jewel (that had been in his hand) into/within that house.

sentences.s523-s25: The same moon-faced ones alighted on another leaf, and at once they sat down and went off.
sentences.s523-s26: From the portico he turned his face toward Pars; he went on, joyful of heart and seeking the road.
sentences.s523-s17: He remained until night rose from the mountain; Ardavan lay down to sleep, and the place became without company.
sentences.s523-s18: He came out of the iwan and, like an arrow in his bearing, brought the jewel to Ardashir.
sentences.s523-s19: He saw the world-seeker, a cup in his hand; the horse-keepers were all asleep and drunk.
sentences.s523-s20: Where had Ardashir made them drunk, that he was thus compelled to go?
sentences.s523-s1: When the face of the realm became like tar, the young maidservant came to Ardashir.
sentences.s523-s2: When the sea was stirred, the young man was roused—he who for a day had no patience with Ardavan.
sentences.s523-s3: The maidservant spoke what the bright‑souled one had been saying to renowned Ardavan.
sentences.s523-s4: When he heard the words from Golnār in that way, he chose patience and silence.
sentences.s523-s9: He answered thus: "I am your servant; I will not be apart from you as long as I live."
sentences.s523-s10: He kept saying, his lips full of the cold wind; yellow water poured from his eyes.
sentences.s523-s11: He said this to the moon‑faced Ardashir: that tomorrow it will inevitably have to happen.
sentences.s523-s12: The little maid came to her own iwan, having placed her body and soul in her palm.
sentences.s523-s21: He had selected two noble steeds and placed them by the manger; they were thus beneath the saddle.
sentences.s523-s22: When the world-seeker beheld Golnār's face, he saw that jewel and a red dinar.
sentences.s523-s23: At the same time he set a cup before them and struck the bridle on the heads of the Arabian horses.
sentences.s523-s24: They lay down to sleep, and he himself sat up, a poisoned sword in his hand.
sentences.s523-s5: The man's heart became keen as the moon; from that moment onward he kept seeking a way of escape.
sentences.s523-s6: He said to him, 'If I go to Iran, from Ray I will set out toward the city of the brave.'
sentences.s523-s7: You, come with me and set the hounds (or make the hunt) so that you will be on the road; if you remain here, you will be near the king.
sentences.s523-s8: If you come with me you will grow wealthy; indeed you will become the helmet (i.e., the commander) over the realm.
sentences.s523-s13: When the face of the world turned yellow from the sun, the lapis‑blue night came into the curve.
sentences.s523-s14: The maid opened the treasures and began to search through every jewel.
sentences.s523-s15: He had as much use for rubies and splendid jewels as he did for dinars.
sentences.s523-s16: He came to a place where he sat down, and in that house he set the jewel down from his hand.
